name: Ahura Mazda
shortDescription: Ahura Mazda is the supreme god in Zoroastrianism, representing goodness, light, and truth. He is the uncreated creator of all things and the ultimate source of cosmic order.
description: Ahura Mazda, meaning "Wise Lord," is the central figure in Zoroastrianism, embodying ultimate goodness, wisdom, and cosmic order (Asha). He is considered the uncreated creator of the universe, the source of all light and truth, and the opponent of Angra Mainyu (Ahriman), the principle of evil and darkness. Ahura Mazda's existence is eternal and transcendent, and he is believed to be omniscient, omnipresent, and omnipotent. The struggle between Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu forms the core of Zoroastrian cosmology, a cosmic battle between good and evil that will ultimately result in the triumph of Ahura Mazda and the restoration of the world to its original perfection. Concepts like justice, truth, and impartiality are intrinsically linked to Ahura Mazda. At the Chinvat Bridge, the bridge of judgement, souls are judged based on their adherence to Asha, the divine order established by Ahura Mazda. Those who lived righteously are guided to paradise, while the wicked are cast into hell. Mithra and Sraosha are important yazatas (divine beings) associated with Ahura Mazda, aiding him in his cosmic battle and acting as intermediaries between the divine and human realms. The concept of Asha, the divine order, is also closely tied to Ahura Mazda, representing the principles of truth, righteousness, and cosmic harmony that he embodies and upholds.

sources.title: The Religion of the Good Religion
sources.author: Werner Sundermann, Almut Hintze, François de Blois

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda created the world in stages, first the spiritual (menog) and then the material (getig). He created the first humans, animals, and plants to establish Asha and combat the forces of Angra Mainyu.

keyMyths.title: The Cosmic Battle with Angra Mainyu
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda is perpetually engaged in a cosmic battle with Angra Mainyu (Ahriman), the embodiment of evil. This struggle shapes the universe and human existence, with humans playing a role in choosing between good and evil.

keyMyths.title: The Final Judgment at the Chinvat Bridge
keyMyths.description: After death, the soul journeys to the Chinvat Bridge, where Ahura Mazda's divine judgment determines its fate. Those who have lived according to Asha will cross safely into paradise, while the wicked will fall into hell.

keyMyths.title: The Appearance of Zoroaster
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda revealed himself to Zoroaster, choosing him as his prophet to spread the message of truth and righteousness. Zoroaster received divine inspiration and teachings directly from Ahura Mazda, which he then shared with humanity.

keyMyths.title: The Frashokereti (Renovation of the World)
keyMyths.description: At the end of time, Ahura Mazda will ultimately triumph over Angra Mainyu, leading to the Frashokereti, the renovation of the world. Evil will be vanquished, and the world will be restored to its original perfection, free from suffering and death.

extendedContent.title: Definition and Attributes
extendedContent.content:
Ahura Mazda, literally translating to "Wise Lord," represents the supreme deity in Zoroastrianism. He is not merely a powerful being, but the embodiment of all that is good, wise, and just. He is considered the uncreated, eternal, and omniscient creator of the universe, responsible for bringing forth both the spiritual and material realms. His wisdom is not just intellectual prowess, but a profound understanding of the cosmic order (Asha) and the principles that govern existence.

Central to the understanding of Ahura Mazda is his role as the source of light and truth. This light is not just physical illumination, but also spiritual enlightenment, guiding humanity towards righteousness and away from darkness. Truth, in this context, encompasses honesty, integrity, and adherence to the divine principles established by Ahura Mazda. He is the ultimate standard of morality and the guarantor of cosmic justice.

Furthermore, Ahura Mazda is often described as being surrounded by Amesha Spentas, or "Bounteous Immortals." These are not merely angels or servants, but rather emanations or aspects of Ahura Mazda himself, each representing a different virtue or quality, such as Good Mind (Vohu Manah), Righteousness (Asha Vahishta), and Dominion (Khshathra Vairya). These Amesha Spentas assist Ahura Mazda in maintaining the cosmic order and fighting against the forces of evil.

Unlike some other religious traditions, Ahura Mazda is not depicted as a jealous or vengeful God. He is a benevolent and compassionate being who desires the salvation and well-being of all creation. He empowers humanity with free will, allowing them to choose between good and evil, and encourages them to strive towards perfection through righteous thoughts, words, and deeds.

extendedContent.title: Cultural Context and Zoroastrian Cosmology
extendedContent.content:
Ahura Mazda's significance is deeply intertwined with the Zoroastrian worldview, which emphasizes the ongoing cosmic battle between good and evil. He stands as the champion of good, constantly opposing Angra Mainyu (Ahriman), the principle of darkness and chaos. This duality is not a balanced equilibrium, but rather a struggle where Ahura Mazda is ultimately destined to triumph.

In Zoroastrian cosmology, the universe unfolds in stages, beginning with the creation of the spiritual realm, followed by the creation of the material world. Angra Mainyu, however, corrupts the initial perfection of creation, introducing suffering, death, and evil. This sets the stage for the ongoing struggle, where humanity plays a crucial role in choosing between good and evil and contributing to the ultimate victory of Ahura Mazda.

The concept of free will is central to this cosmology. Humans are not mere puppets of divine forces but are empowered to make conscious choices that align with either Ahura Mazda or Angra Mainyu. By choosing righteous thoughts, words, and deeds, individuals strengthen the forces of good and hasten the eventual triumph of light over darkness. This active participation in the cosmic struggle is a fundamental aspect of Zoroastrian belief.

The Zoroastrian worldview also includes a belief in a final judgment, where individuals will be held accountable for their actions. Those who have aligned themselves with Ahura Mazda and lived righteously will be rewarded with eternal bliss, while those who have embraced evil will face punishment. This emphasis on personal responsibility and the consequences of one's actions reinforces the importance of striving for goodness and resisting the temptations of evil.

extendedContent.title: Related Practices and Ethical Implications
extendedContent.content:
The belief in Ahura Mazda as the embodiment of goodness and truth profoundly shapes Zoroastrian ethical principles and practices. Zoroastrians are encouraged to live a life dedicated to righteousness, truthfulness, and compassion. This translates into concrete actions, such as honesty in business dealings, kindness towards others, and respect for the environment.

The Zoroastrian emphasis on good thoughts, good words, and good deeds (Humata, Hukhta, Hvarshta) forms the cornerstone of their ethical framework. Individuals are expected to cultivate positive thoughts, speak truthfully and kindly, and perform actions that benefit themselves, their communities, and the world around them. This holistic approach to ethics emphasizes the interconnectedness of thought, word, and action.

Fire plays a central role in Zoroastrian worship, symbolizing Ahura Mazda's light and purity. Fire temples are places of communal worship, where priests maintain a sacred flame that is never allowed to be extinguished. The fire is not worshipped as a deity itself, but rather serves as a focal point for prayer and meditation, reminding believers of the divine presence and the importance of maintaining inner purity.

Zoroastrianism also emphasizes the importance of preserving the environment and treating all living beings with respect. This stems from the belief that the earth and all its creatures are part of Ahura Mazda's creation and should be cherished and protected. Traditional Zoroastrian practices, such as the careful disposal of the dead through exposure to the elements (dakhma), reflect this reverence for nature and the desire to avoid polluting the earth.

worshipPractices.name: Fire Temples
worshipPractices.description: Fire is a central symbol of Ahura Mazda's divine light and purity. Zoroastrians maintain eternal flames in fire temples as a focal point for worship, representing Ahura Mazda's presence and the ongoing battle against darkness.

worshipPractices.name: Prayers and Hymns (Yasna)
worshipPractices.description: Devotees recite prayers and hymns from the Avesta, the Zoroastrian scripture, to honor Ahura Mazda and seek his guidance. The Yasna ceremony is a particularly important ritual involving offerings and recitations performed by priests.

festivals.name: Nowruz (Persian New Year)
festivals.description: While not exclusively dedicated to Ahura Mazda, Nowruz has Zoroastrian roots and emphasizes themes of renewal, light overcoming darkness, and the triumph of good over evil, aligning with Ahura Mazda's principles. Celebrations involve setting a Haft-Sin table with symbolic items and family gatherings.

festivals.name: Jashne Sadeh
festivals.description: This festival celebrates the discovery of fire and light, symbolizing the victory of good over evil. A large bonfire is lit, and communities gather to sing, dance, and share food, reinforcing the values of hope and perseverance.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ahura Mazda's emphasis on good deeds, ethical conduct, and the ongoing struggle between good and evil continues to influence modern ethical frameworks and philosophical discussions. The concept of a singular, benevolent creator God has also resonated in other religious and spiritual traditions, leaving a lasting impact on comparative theology and religious studies.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Zeus, like Ahura Mazda, is the supreme god, associated with cosmic order, justice, and wisdom, wielding power against chaotic forces.
Ra, the sun god, embodies light, creation, and the triumph over darkness, mirroring Ahura Mazda's role as the source of light and good against opposing forces.
Odin, while complex, represents wisdom, knowledge, and the pursuit of understanding to maintain cosmic balance, a characteristic aligned with Ahura Mazda's wise and orderly nature.
Vishnu is the preserver and maintainer of cosmic order (dharma), constantly working to restore balance and defeat evil, similar to Ahura Mazda's fight against Angra Mainyu.

associations.name: Light
associations.significance: Ahura Mazda is the source of all light, both physical and spiritual, representing enlightenment and knowledge.

associations.name: Order
associations.significance: Ahura Mazda embodies cosmic order (Asha), maintaining balance and harmony in the universe.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: The sun is a visual representation of Ahura Mazda's radiant presence and life-giving energy.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White symbolizes purity, goodness, and the absence of evil, reflecting Ahura Mazda's nature.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Ahura Mazda is the singular, supreme creator and ruler of the universe in Zoroastrian monotheism.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Fire represents Ahura Mazda's divine presence, purity, and transformative power.

associations.name: Wisdom
associations.significance: Ahura Mazda, meaning 'Wise Lord', embodies ultimate wisdom and knowledge in all things.

companions.name: Spenta Mainyu
companions.description: Spenta Mainyu is the Holy Spirit or Bounteous Spirit, often considered an aspect or emanation of Ahura Mazda himself. He represents the positive and creative force in the universe, working to uphold Asha.

companions.name: Mithra
companions.description: Mithra is a divinity associated with covenants, oaths, and the rising sun, often seen as a mediator between Ahura Mazda and humanity. He is a powerful ally in the fight against evil.

companions.name: Sraosha
companions.description: Sraosha is the Yazata of obedience, discipline, and divine service. He acts as a messenger of Ahura Mazda and helps guide souls on the path of righteousness.

companions.name: Vohu Manah
companions.description: Vohu Manah, meaning 'Good Purpose' or 'Good Mind,' is one of the Amesha Spentas, representing divine wisdom and good intentions. He is a close companion and advisor to Ahura Mazda.

companions.name: Atar
companions.description: Atar is the Zoroastrian divinity representing fire, a symbol of purity, light, and Ahura Mazda's presence. He is considered a helper in rituals and a protector against evil.
